output:
{
  "status": "completed",
  "perspective": {
    "briefSummary": "This project investigates the technical validity and inevitability of 'model collapse'—the degradation of generative model performance when trained on recursively generated synthetic data. The research will synthesize current machine learning literature to determine if collapse is a fundamental mathematical certainty or a manageable engineering challenge. By evaluating the mechanisms of distribution drift and the efficacy of mitigation strategies like data mixing and 'forgetting' prevention, the system will distinguish between theoretical worst-case scenarios and empirical realities observed in large-scale training loops.",
    "interpretedIntent": "To determine the technical boundary conditions under which recursive synthetic training becomes unsustainable and to identify which specific architectural or data-curation interventions effectively prevent the loss of tail-end distribution diversity.",
    "inferredResearchFrame": "Model collapse is not an absolute law but a function of data-to-noise ratios and training hygiene; the research tests the hypothesis that strategic integration of 'fresh' real-world data and specific sampling techniques can maintain model stability indefinitely.",
    "evidenceForCriteria": [
      "Empirical studies showing a decrease in statistical variance or 'mode collapse' over successive generations.",
      "Mathematical proofs demonstrating cumulative approximation errors in recursive training loops.",
      "Results showing that synthetic data amplifies existing biases or erases low-probability 'tail' events.",
      "Experiments where model performance degrades even with high-quality synthetic inputs."
    ],
    "evidenceAgainstCriteria": [
      "Studies demonstrating stable performance over many generations when using 'data-mixing' (real + synthetic) ratios.",
      "Evidence that advanced sampling techniques (e.g., top-p, temperature scaling) or architectural regularizers delay collapse.",
      "Empirical findings where synthetic data actually improves performance on specific downstream tasks (e.g., reasoning or coding).",
      "Research showing that 'curated' synthetic data (filtered by a stronger model) avoids the pitfalls of raw recursive training."
    ],
    "subquestions": [
      "What are the specific mathematical mechanisms (e.g., statistical approximation error, functional collapse) identified as the primary drivers of degradation?",
      "What is the critical threshold or ratio of real-to-synthetic data required to maintain distributional stability?",
      "How do different generative modalities (LLMs vs. Diffusion Models) differ in their susceptibility to recursive collapse?",
      "Which mitigation strategies (e.g., data provenance tracking, replay buffers, or synthetic data filtering) show the most robust empirical success?",
      "To what extent does the 'model capacity' of the student vs. the teacher influence the speed of collapse?"
    ]
  },
  "artifacts": {
    "overview": "# Project Overview: Model Collapse From Recursive Synthetic Data Training\n\n### 1. Research Question\nUnder what conditions does recursive training on synthetic data lead to model collapse in generative models?\n\n### 2. Research Objective\nThis synthesis evaluates the technical validity and inevitability of \"model collapse\"—the degradation of generative model performance when trained on recursively generated synthetic data. The objective is to determine the boundary conditions under which recursive synthetic training becomes unsustainable and to identify specific architectural or data-curation interventions that effectively prevent the loss of tail-end distribution diversity.\n\n### 3. Research Frame and Working Hypothesis\nThe research operates under the hypothesis that model collapse is not an absolute law but a function of data-to-noise ratios and training hygiene. The project tests the premise that the strategic integration of \"fresh\" real-world data and specific sampling techniques can maintain model stability, distinguishing between theoretical worst-case scenarios and empirical realities observed in large-scale training loops.\n\n### 4. Scope Boundaries\n*   **In-Scope:** Machine learning literature concerning recursive training, synthetic data reuse, distribution degradation, and mathematical proofs of error accumulation.\n*   **Out-of-Scope:** Product-level commentary, non-technical blog posts, and speculative industry opinion pieces.\n*   **Temporal Focus:** 2023–2025.\n*   **Geographic Focus:** Global academic and industrial research output.\n\n### 5. Source Inventory\nThe following sources have been synthesized to address the research question. *Note: Provenance is categorized as \"Discovered\" (retrieved via literature search) or \"Uploaded\" (provided by the user).*\n\n| Source ID | Provenance | Description |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| SOURCE 1 | Discovered | Study on the limits of data acc
...[truncated]
